narrative_blocks.header: Died at eight, third of Delia's four
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin R Van Horn was born October 6, 1830 in Chicopee MA and died August 13, 1839 at age 8. He was the third child of Delia Stiles Van Horn and Reuel Van Horn, and the only one of their four children not to reach adulthood. His brother George W Van Horne, born three years after Benjamin's death, would never know him.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at eight, third of Delia's four
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin R Van Horn was born October 6, 1830 in Chicopee MA and died August 13, 1839 at age 8. He was the third child of Delia Stiles Van Horn and Reuel Van Horn, and the only one of their four children not to reach adulthood. His brother George W Van Horne, born three years after Benjamin's death, would never know him.

career.notes: Descendant of Born Van Horn of Saxe-Coburg, Germany (per Stiles book extended descent of Delia).

narrative_blocks.header: Longest-lived of nine, a widow for decades
narrative_blocks.body: Delia Stiles Van Horn outlived all eight of her Stiles siblings and survived her husband Ruel, a Chicopee farmer, by twenty-four years. She lived to eighty-two.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son was Lincoln’s consul at Marseilles
narrative_blocks.body: Her son George W. Van Horn was appointed United States Consul at Marseilles by President Lincoln and served through 1866. He later founded a newspaper in Iowa and wrote novels.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 461
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), Samuel Stiles entry 233
Historic Northampton, Gare Collection: Samuel Stiles silvermaking entry

notable.primary_url_label: Historic Northampton: Samuel Stiles, the Gare Collection of Silvermaking and Watch Repair Tools

military_service.notes: Served in a company formed in Windsor CT during the War of 1812 with Britain. Stationed at Fort Trumbull (New London CT) in February 1813.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 private, stationed Fort Trumbull
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles served as a private in a company formed in Windsor CT during the War of 1812 with Britain, stationed at Fort Trumbull (New London) in February 1813. He was 50 years old at the time — old for a militiaman — but the Connecticut coast was being threatened by British naval blockade, and middle-aged men were drawn into local defense.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on his thirty-ninth wedding anniversary
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles married Hannah Ellsworth in Windsor on October 15, 1787; he died at Windsor on October 15, 1826 — exactly thirty-nine years later, on their wedding anniversary. Hannah survived him by only fifteen months, dying at Chicopee MA in January 1828.

narrative_blocks.header: Three states across forty years of marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel and Hannah's life together threaded across three states: their first three children were born in Northampton MA (1788-1791); the next six in Chester MA (1793-1802); and they finally returned to Samuel's native Windsor CT where he died in 1826. The pattern reflects the era's flexible Connecticut-Massachusetts borderland economy.

narrative_blocks.header: Northampton silversmith — surviving spoons in Deerfield
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles opened a clock and watch shop on Shop Row in Northampton in 1785 — a few rods south of the Court-House — and worked as a clockmaker, watchmaker, jeweler, and silversmith for six years before partnering with Jedidiah Baldwin in July 1791. Two silver spoons bearing his 'SS' maker's mark survive in the collection of Historic Deerfield. The partnership dissolved within a year and Samuel left Northampton for Chester MA in 1792.

career.organization: Samuel Stiles shop, Shop Row, Northampton MA
career.notes: Opened shop in Northampton 1785, a few rods south of the Court-House. Advertised in Springfield Hampshire Herald July 27, 1785. Made silver including spoons with maker's mark 'SS' (two examples in Historic Deerfield collection). Took apprentices Joel Brown and Horace Clark, c.1789-1793.

Partnership with Jedidiah Baldwin (1769-1849), formed July 20, 1791 in shop nearly opposite the Meeting-house in Northampton — clock and watch business, jewelry, all branches of goldsmith's business. Baldwin had served apprenticeship to Thomas Harland of Norwich CT. Partnership lasted approximately one year before Samuel left Northampton.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Not in JSON.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Maiden name not documented in EH 461. Not in JSON.

narrative_blocks.header: Windsor Ellsworth family — Chief Justice was kin
narrative_blocks.body: She was a daughter of Jonathan Ellsworth of Windsor Connecticut, of the same Windsor Ellsworth family that produced Oliver Ellsworth (1745-1807) — third Chief Justice of the United States and co-author of the Connecticut Compromise. Specific kinship between Hannah's father and Oliver has not yet been documented but the Windsor Ellsworth naming patterns place them in the same kin network.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at Chicopee Massachusetts after husband Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: She survived her husband Samuel Stiles by fifteen months — Samuel died at Chicopee Massachusetts in October 1826, Hannah followed there on 12 January 1828. The Connecticut-born couple's migration to Chicopee tracked their daughter Delia, who lived there.
